work.reflection: Getting to lead backend development has been genuinely exciting - it's given me the space to implement approaches I've long believed in, and to focus on the engineering rather than the logistics around it. Designing the architecture from scratch to help bring ambitious game ideas to life is exactly the kind of challenge I didn't know I'd love as much as I do.
details: I design the backend in Go for what a small team can actually carry, not for the cleverest possible solution.
Made the call to move our whole platform onto Railway: the lesson that stuck wasn't about tooling, it was that the right constraint to design around is almost always the team, not the technology.
I direct and verify AI rather than hand-write code, which forces me to know exactly what correct looks like before I can trust the output.

work.reflection: Xero was my first real encounter with truly untouched legacy systems - battle-tested software that had been quietly running for years with nobody daring to go near it. Learning to extend those systems carefully, gradually threading new functionality into old foundations without disturbing what already worked, taught me a kind of patience and precision I hadn't needed before.
details: Extending undocumented C# and IIS systems nobody wanted to touch taught me to change code I didn't write without breaking what was quietly depending on it.
Helped design the modern .NET replacement while the legacy platform kept running - I learned how much risk really lives in a migration.
Being on-call for incidents well outside my own squad's scope made me comfortable reasoning about unfamiliar systems under real pressure.

work.reflection: Coming out of university into the corporate world was a real adjustment - and honestly, a great one - learning to connect what I was building to business outcomes completely changed how I think about code. It shifted my whole frame from 'what's the clever solution' to 'what actually helps' - a perspective shift that was genuinely hard to align with at first.
details: My first role out of university, in a TypeScript microservices ecosystem across a team of 10-20 - where I learned the difference between code that works and code that fits a team, and started writing for the second.
Building integrations with third-party vendors and legacy platforms taught me that the messy, real-world constraints usually are the engineering problem, not a distraction from it.

work.reflection: Raygun was my first real look at production software outside of university - building a developer-facing crash reporting library exposed me to a world of decisions and constraints I hadn't encountered before. Without the business perspective I'd develop later, a lot of the decision making was hard to follow at this time, but seeing how software actually lived and breathed outside of academia was fascinating.
details: Designed and shipped raygun4reactnative, a published React Native library for crash reporting and real-user monitoring - my first time building something other developers would actually depend on.

interests.name: Ergo Keyboards
interests.summary: A childhood elbow injury left me with limited rotation in my right wrist - not a problem in school, but consistent full-time work at a keyboard changed that. I started with proper touch typing and a company-provided ergonomic keyboard, fell into keyboard-centric workflows and Neovim, and from there went deep into the world of ergonomic keyboards trying to solve a growing list of problems. A Planck EZ, a Glove80, a period of defeat, and a standard keyboard later - I landed on the ZSA Voyager, which I now have mounted to the side of my desk. Being able to rest my elbow properly again has made a real difference, and whilst I'm still a keyboard-first person, I've made peace with the mouse too.
interests.cvSummary: Deep into ergonomic keyboards following a childhood elbow injury - currently on a ZSA Voyager after a long journey through ortholinear and split boards.

interests.name: Linux
interests.summary: Linux is my daily driver - once I was no longer tied to Windows for work, making the switch was an easy call. After years of fighting developer-unfriendly edge cases and working around the OS rather than with it, the speed and control Linux gives me felt like a completely different world.
interests.cvSummary: Made the full switch to Fedora Linux once I was no longer tied to Windows for work - haven't considered going back.
